frameworks.name: neuromodulatory gain control of network dynamics
frameworks.one_line: catecholamines reshape forebrain network state via receptor-targeted modulation

frameworks.name: spatially homogeneous catecholaminergic modulation
frameworks.one_line: view that catecholamine effects on cortical dynamics are uniform across the brain

frameworks.name: receptor-expression-guided functional mapping
frameworks.one_line: linking fMRI fluctuation patterns to spatial maps of receptor gene expression

frameworks.name: linear decomposition of resting-state fMRI
frameworks.one_line: data-driven separation of drug-modulated correlated signal fluctuations

core_question: Are catecholaminergic effects on brain-wide network dynamics spatially homogeneous or structured by receptor distributions?
key_assumptions: catecholamine receptors with distinct functional effects have heterogeneous cortical density
fMRI signal covariation reflects network-level dynamics
receptor gene expression maps index regional receptor density
manipulations: atomoxetine versus placebo in humans at rest
linear decomposition contrasting drug-increased and drug-decreased fluctuation patterns
key_findings: two distinct spatial fluctuation patterns showed reliable opposite drug effects
one pattern decreased and one increased under atomoxetine
pattern structure matched α1 adrenergic, D2-like dopamine, and β adrenergic receptor expression maps
β receptor maps correlated with both patterns but with opposite signs
interpretations: catecholaminergic forebrain effects are spatially more structured than homogeneous models assume
receptor-specific architecture shapes drug-induced network-state changes
connecting_signals: α1 and β adrenergic receptor maps
